name: Iraq
summary: Day 59 Sunday. WSJ ATTRIBUTION ARCHITECTURE UPGRADED — APRIL 12 SAUDI MoFA PROTEST SUMMONS NOW CONFIRMED + QAANI-BAGHDAD VISIT REPORTED + NAMED MILITIAS KATAIB HEZBOLLAH/ASAIB AHL AL-HAQ. Three operational details surface beyond the WSJ April 21 'half of ~1,000 drone attacks from Iraq' piece: (1) On April 12, 2026, the Saudi Ministry of Foreign Affairs formally summoned Iraq's ambassador to Riyadh and issued a protest note warning that Saudi Arabia 'would take necessary measures to ensure its national security and territorial integrity' (House of Saud, Defense News). Saudi MoFA has not denied. CONFIRMED diplomatic action. (2) Gen. Esmail Qaani — IRGC overseas-operations commander (Quds Force successor to Soleimani) — reportedly visited Baghdad during the period of escalation. REPORTED, indicates direct Tehran-Baghdad militia command coordination. (3) Specific militias named: Kataib Hezbollah and Asaib Ahl al-Haq (post-2003 US-invasion origin). Targets named: Yanbu oil hub on Red Sea, multiple oil installations in Saudi Eastern Province, Kuwait's only civilian airport, sites in Bahrain. UNN April relays the WSJ framing as a 'covert war' between Saudi Arabia and Iraq — strategic-narrative-level reframing that elevates the Saudi-Iraq bilateral from 'drone-attack incident series' to 'low-intensity conflict' classification. Iraqi government has not publicly addressed the Saudi protest summons (silence is data); Iran's state media has not addressed the Qaani-Baghdad visit. The diplomatic-protest substrate is CONFIRMED; the 50% attribution share remains REPORTED. Hormuz closure persists; Iraq's southern oil exports remain constrained — production ~900K bpd vs pre-war 3.3M bpd. Trump cancellation of Witkoff-Kushner dispatch removes the near-term Iraq-restart contingency.

signals.content: Iranian drones struck coalition military camps near Erbil and Baghdad, wounding US soldiers.
Iraq declared force majeure on all foreign-operated oilfields due to Hormuz disruption. Basra Oil Company production cut from 3.3M to 900K bpd. Iraq depends on crude for 90%+ of income.

Force majeure on all foreign-operated oilfields persists — Day 17 with no resolution. Production remains at 900K bpd (down from 3.3M). Iraqi health authorities report 109 people killed from the conflict. Southern oil exports through Hormuz remain blocked. No significant new Iraq-specific developments but the force majeure continues as a structural supply constraint removing 2.4M bpd from global markets.

Iraqi-linked oil tanker exited Hormuz via Iran on April 5 per Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker — Iraq is in Iran's three-tier 'friendly' category. Iraq's PMF (Iran-aligned militias) is still part of the Iran-led 'all fronts' framework that Tehran's 10-point counter demands include. Iraq remains structurally Iran-aligned within the diplomatic framework — Iran's demand for end of war 'across all fronts including resistance groups' implicitly covers Iraqi PMF.

IRAQ DIRECT BENEFICIARY OF CEASEFIRE — FORCE MAJEURE PATH TO RESTART [REPORTED]. Iraq declared force majeure on all foreign-operated oilfields March 20, cutting production from 3.3M to 900K bpd (a ~2.4M bpd loss). 90%+ of Iraqi income is from oil. The IRGC three-tier system had already granted Iraq a 'brotherly' exemption — Iraqi crude tankers were transiting Hormuz under Iranian permission. The 14-day ceasefire creates the path for Iraq to formally lift force majeure and restart production: (a) reduces operator risk premium for foreign companies, (b) reduces shipping/insurance costs, (c) provides political cover for a coordinated restart. CRITICAL MECHANISM: Iraq's restart is the largest single piece of supply restoration available globally — bigger than OPEC+'s 206K bpd, bigger than US SPR releases. If 1.5M-2.0M bpd of Iraqi production comes back online over 2-4 weeks, that's the biggest bearish oil signal of the year and walks back another $5-10/bbl of war premium. Watch for: (a) Iraqi oil ministry statements over the next 48 hours, (b) BP/Eni/CNPC operational updates, (c) Basra port loading data, (d) coordination with Saudi Yanbu bypass (the 40+ anchored VLCCs at Yanbu would be the staging area for Iraqi exports if Basra/Hormuz routes restart). Iraq is the silent largest beneficiary of the ceasefire.

Iranian-backed Iraqi militias continue drone attacks on Gulf states DESPITE ceasefire [CONFIRMED]. Bahrain intercepted seven drones from Iraqi militias and summoned Iraq's charge d'affaires (source: ISW Special Report April 13, The National). This means Iran has NOT ordered its proxy partners to cease attacks on regional states, even while the Iran-US ceasefire formally holds. The simultaneity of negotiate + proxy attacks continue is itself the signal per project methodology — Iran maintains pressure through proxies while keeping formal bilateral ceasefire intact. Iraq's own force majeure persists with production at 900K bpd. The US naval blockade intensifies Iraq's export challenges through Hormuz.

HORMUZ REOPENING → IRAQ EXPORT RESTART WINDOW [REPORTED]. Iraqi News and Reuters report ~6M barrels of combined Iraqi and Saudi crude exited Hormuz in the days following Iran FM Araghchi's April 17 'completely open' declaration. Basra Oil Company chief stated Iraq can restore exports to pre-war 3.4M bpd within a week if Hormuz stays open (USNews exclusive). Ministry of Oil expected to formally lift force majeure on southern loading terminals if ceasefire endures through T+3 (April 21 expiry). Operational mechanics: (a) reduced operator risk premium for BP/Eni/CNPC/ExxonMobil partners, (b) lower war-risk insurance, (c) political cover for coordinated restart. This is the single largest restorable supply globally — a ~2.4M bpd swing dwarfs OPEC+'s 206K bpd May adjustment. If confirmed, walks back $5-10/bbl of remaining war premium. Watch: Iraqi oil ministry formal statement, Basra port loading data, Saudi Yanbu bypass coordination. Caveat: dated Brent physical still ~$132 vs futures $91.87 — $40 gap signals physical has NOT yet reconverged.

WSJ VIA ISW: ~HALF OF ~1,000 DRONE ATTACKS ON SAUDI DURING WAR CAME FROM IRAN-BACKED IRAQI MILITIAS [REPORTED — single WSJ-source via unnamed officials]. Wall Street Journal April 21 reported, citing unnamed sources, that Iranian-backed Iraqi militias conducted approximately half of the ~1,000 drone attacks targeting Saudi Arabia during the war (relayed in ISW Critical Threats Iran Update April 21-22). Historical-cumulative claim, not fresh kinetic; operationally significant because (1) materially upgrades the implied weight of the Iraq → Iran-axis edge in the graph (Iraq has been Layer 2), and (2) provides a cleaner mechanism for the Saudi anxiety the Saudi Tadawul -1.2% Friday close had been pricing. Single-sourced (WSJ unnamed officials) — should remain REPORTED until either Saudi MoD or CENTCOM independently confirms attribution. Iraqi government has not addressed the claim Saturday. Iranian state media silent; Saudi MoD silent — all three silences are intelligence (each side has reasons not to confirm or deny).
sources: ISW Critical Threats Iran Update April 21-22
signals.verification: REPORTED single WSJ-sources via unnamed officials, ISW relay.

signals.content: WSJ IRAQ-DRONE ATTRIBUTION UPGRADED: APRIL 12 SAUDI MoFA PROTEST SUMMONS CONFIRMED + QAANI-BAGHDAD VISIT REPORTED + KATAIB HEZBOLLAH/ASAIB AHL AL-HAQ NAMED [CONFIRMED diplomatic action + REPORTED command-coordination + REPORTED specific militias]. Three operational details: (1) April 12, 2026, Saudi Ministry of Foreign Affairs formally summoned Iraq's ambassador to Riyadh and issued protest note warning Saudi Arabia 'would take necessary measures to ensure its national security and territorial integrity' — CONFIRMED via House of Saud + Defense News; Saudi MoFA has not denied. (2) Gen. Esmail Qaani — IRGC overseas-operations commander — reportedly visited Baghdad during the period of escalation, indicating direct Tehran-Baghdad militia command coordination — REPORTED single-source via WSJ. (3) Specific militias named: Kataib Hezbollah and Asaib Ahl al-Haq, originating post-2003 US invasion. Targets: Yanbu oil hub on Red Sea, multiple oil installations in Saudi Eastern Province, Kuwait's only civilian airport, sites in Bahrain. UNN April relays the WSJ framing as a 'covert war' between Saudi Arabia and Iraq — strategic-narrative-level reframing. Iraqi government has not publicly addressed the Saudi protest summons. Materially upgrades graph encoding: April 12 Saudi-Iraq diplomatic protest is CONFIRMED, supports stronger iraq → saudi-arabia edge weight; Qaani-Baghdad visit reinforces iran → iraq command linkage. (Sources: WSJ, ISW Critical Threats April 21-22, House of Saud, Defense News, UNN, Long War Journal)
signals.verification: CONFIRMED April 12 Saudi MoFA protest summons. REPORTED on Qaani-Baghdad visit and 50% attribution share.

edges.context: 2.4M bpd production offline due to force majeure since March 20. Day 17 with no resolution. Additional supply shock on top of Iranian production losses and Hormuz closure.
Iraq's southern oil exports depend on Hormuz transit. Closure made exports impossible, forcing force majeure.

Day 59 Sunday. Architecture upgraded: Qaani-Baghdad visit REPORTED indicates direct Tehran-Baghdad militia command coordination; named militias Kataib Hezbollah, Asaib Ahl al-Haq. Reinforces iran → iraq command linkage. Iranian state silence on Qaani visit is consistent with state suppression of operational details.

Day 59 Sunday. April 12 Saudi MoFA formal protest summons of Iraqi ambassador now CONFIRMED (House of Saud, Defense News); WSJ April 21 'half of ~1,000 drone attacks' attribution still REPORTED. UNN frames the bilateral as 'covert war' — strategic-narrative-level reframing. Saudi MoFA acting through diplomatic-protocol channels rather than public messaging. Cleaner mechanism for Saudi Tadawul anxiety than direct Iranian kinetic.

current.restart_potential: 1.5-2.0M bpd over 2-4 weeks if FM lifted
